cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. The story is about Sir Arthur Conan Doyle meeting a taxi driver who correctly guesses his identity. The question is asking why Doyle was surprised. The key part here is the setup. The driver turns to Doyle and addresses him by name, "Mr. Doyle," before Doyle even says a word. Then when Doyle asks if the driver had ever seen him before, the driver says no, but then explains all the clues that led him to that conclusion. The main point of surprise would be that the driver knew who he was without having met him before. Looking at the options: (A) the driver had seen him before. But the driver says he hasn't, so this is probably not the answer. (B) the driver could call him by his name. That seems to be what surprised Doyle. The driver knew his name without being told. (C) the driver was waiting for him. The story says the driver was at the taxi stop where people returning from Marseilles come, but there's no mention of waiting specifically for him. (D) the driver answered no questions from him. The driver does answer questions, explaining his reasoning. So this doesn't fit.
